speaker: interviewer
text: Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. This is roll number 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] But then came times when they started shipping people, where the transports stopped coming to Auschwitz. The-- and the trains began to go the other way, loaded up with people, nobody knew where. One day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we heard there is an engineer from a factory. He needs 200 girls. I went. The whole group of girls with whom we slept went. Me, he put aside, because of my eyeglasses. He was the one and only person in Auschwitz [LAUGHS] that noticed my glasses. And he says, he cannot use me. I am young, he said, yes. But I cannot use you because I need precise work, and you can't do it with your glasses on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was left behind. But, you know, then things started look-- to look up. Things started to turn in a different vein. I was transferred to block 27, where I met other girls my age. And I was given a job by the kapo of B2B to work by the kitchen. I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I'm standing there and then, I allowed somebody to pick up a potato. Because my job was to make sure that the garbage from the kitchen was taken away on daily basis, put on a little lorry, and hauled away. I did not do the hauling. The-- the men used to come for it. But I made sure that it's all in one area, that it's not spread out, that nobody is digging into it. That kapo passed by and saw somebody running away from there with a potato. And she came over to me and slapped me across my face, that I think till this day [LAUGHS] I feel it. But-- and then when she saw me when I was walking back to my place, to my bungalow, to my barrack, she went with me. She stopped me on the way. And she says, I had to do it. The SS woman saw it, too. So I don't want you to get killed. That means she became friendly with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] The spoon that I showed you before was given to me by a Russian woman who was being taken on a transport somewheres else to work. She says, you can use it. I don't need it anymore. I might get another one, or maybe I'll die. So she gave me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then another Russian woman used to come and pick, dig from work, and come straight to me and pick up a potato or two. Or if I hid one for her, and then she brought me a-- a piece of sugar. Because I developed a terrible cold, a terrible cough. And she said to me in Russian, the gist of it was, girl, take care on your health. How can I take care on my health when there is nothing to be take-- with nothing? But she kept on bringing me the sugar on daily basis. Maybe it helped. I don't know. Whatever it was, or maybe my young years pulled me through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And life kept on going on until January 18, 1945, when an order came we have to evacuate and we have to go. You know, hindsight is very-- very good when you know it afterwards. But if I would've known that there won't be no Appel, that there won't be no counting, that there will nothing be, maybe I would've taken the courage to hide in that barracks somewheres, and not to be discovered. But again, I was scared of dogs. I was-- till this day, I'm afraid of dogs, after what I had seen being done by dogs to people. I said, any death-- any death, but not that kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all went. We went like sheeps, maybe, I don't know. We were in it together, and we had to keep on going. We went on a death march. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For three days and three nights we walked. Only one night we spent in somebody's-- in a farmer's barn, because I guess the Nazis wanted to have a little rest, too. And I fell asleep standing up, holding onto a pole in that barn-- I remember that-- maybe for about a half hour or an hour. That was my sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we walked three days, three nights, and we arrived to Gleiwitz train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gleiwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gleiwitz. That was-- Gliwice, where open coal wagons were waiting for us. We got onto it, on the snow, on the ice that was under our feet. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And again, we were loaded up like hundred, like a cattle. There was no way of sitting, no way of standing. Whatever you did, it was not right. The snow and the drizzle kept on coming down on us. How nobody died from pneumonia, I really don't know. Maybe we carried it with us later on. Maybe some of us died in Bergen-Belsen. We spent 10 days and 10 nights on those trains, until we arrived to Bergen-Belsen, some times in February, or maybe end of January, I don't remember exact dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on these open trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No food. We-- we-- whatever-- the snow was our food, ice and snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: Buckets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you dreaming? No. We were not handled. We are cattle. And at that time we were coal. So we arrived to Bergen-Belsen at night. We're walking, and one of the girls walking with me-- because we always walked by five. And she says, you kept on complaining constantly. You're cold, you're cold. Aren't you-- aren't you cold now? I says, I don't feel anything. When we-- when they brought us to [GERMAN], they made us get undressed. I realized why. My feet were frozen. The shoe, with the stocking, with the skin, came off my toes, my instep, and my heel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I finally reached the barrack in Bergen-Belsen, the SS woman gave one look at my feet. And she says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This one is finished. She cannot stand up any more. She's not coming out on the Appelplatz. And I never stood at an Appelplatz, at an Appel. I was always counted as a body on the barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] by being on that barrack, somehow my feet healed a little. They were green. I knew there was an infection. But there was nothing I can do about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no water to get washed. And if I would have gotten out of that barrack, the SS woman would spot me. Or the even block-- blockova would see me. How comes I'm going to get washed like a fancy lady? I don't even know if there was a-- a place to get washed in Bergen-Belsen. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only remember that [GERMAN] area. And one morning I heard that they need 500 women for work. And I asked the-- whatever it was, I pulled my shoe on, the socks, whatever stocking I had, I pulled it on, and the shoe. And I asked the girl near me, please help me stand up. And she says, you're crazy. I said, I'm not. Anything is better than laying here on that floor, and die here. I'd rather die on a train. I'd rather die in the crematorium. But I don't want to die here. And if I stay here, I'll be a dead duck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow I walked, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people began to follow me. And by the time we reached that area, about 60 girls were in back of me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is when I-- when I walked out of that barrack, that's when I saw the piles, the-- of bodies. And my only thoughts were, who has the strength to pick up a dead body-- although we weighed very little-- but who had the strength, how many it took to put all the bodies that high up, to pile them up that high? I really-- I have no answer to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we got to that whatever-- the selection was supposed to take place. And that kapo from Auschwitz-Birkenau B2 was doing the selection. She gave a look at me and she says, to me, [SLOVAKIAN]. That's Slovakian. That's Polish also, but [SLOVAKIAN]. And she put all the girls through. Whoever followed me, she put us all through on that-- on the train. And we got two breads. We got a piece of German salami. We got a piece of margarine. We were put on the train 20 into a wagon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a pail of water and a cup to drink. There was on the other side a pail of whatever necessity you have. But you know, we really used-- I mean, we were using-- the necessities weren't that big. We had no food. So we didn't have [LAUGHS] the-- the urge. And after two days, we arrived to Bavaria. Gellenau was the station. And we were walked into a camp that-- which had only four barracks. There were 500 women from Ravensbruck working already, and we were 500 from Bergen-Belsen. We worked-- the factory was actually in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a factory in the mountain. We used to take the elevators four flights down. Unfortunately in Bergen-Belsen typhoid was rampant, and a lot of our women had the germs in them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of the people that came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] succumbed to typhoid. And the German Lageralteste did not know what to do. That's the one that-- the-- the head of the camp. So he took one barrack, and he turned it into a hospital like. One of the women that was with us had some nurse's training years back. And she was the nurse on that barrack. And on daily basis, people were led into that barrack. Because a lot of them had it, as-- including a friend of mine, who lives now in Plainfield, New Jersey. And she was sick. And after work I used to come and bring a drop of water into her mouth, a grain of sugar to put in her mouth, sponge her around a little bit. I took care of her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After I was there four weeks, and on the Appel just before I went to work, I keeled over almost onto the Lageralteste. And he just like held me up with his hand and called two women behind me. He says, take her to the-- to the hospital-- hospital. When my friend saw me coming in, she made room next her. And they put me there. I was two weeks-- for two weeks I was unconscious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How I knew? When I woke up, I had 13 pieces of bread next to me. And my friend told me she took one piece of bread, and-- and got a spoon of sugar for me. Because I-- in that stupor, in that unconsciousness, I knew-- I had a feeling of a few drops of water and a few grains of sugar on my tongue. I remembered that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I woke up. It was a Thursday when I woke up from-- when my fever broke and whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I shared the bread with her, because I'm sure she was hungry. And on Sunday, we had to get up and go into a train ride-- for a train ride, which was a death-- a death train. In which we spend next two weeks, from Sunday to two weeks later Sunday, without-- nobody had food. I had still a couple pieces of bread left. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And that was-- on this we were living. I was sitting-- I still recall I was so cold. In that train, in that wagon, I was so, so cold. I did not know. It took me many years to understand that I was sitting surrounded by dead bodies. I was sitting on dead bodies. I was surrounded with death. Because as a matter of fact, after the two weeks of being shoved between Germany and Slovakia, and one dialect of German and another dialect of German, we were wheeled back and forth, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I was brought to Mauthausen on a Sunday. They opened up the trains. And out of the 1,000 women, about 142 or 132 walked out on their feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, over 850 died on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they, yeah. They died on that train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they separated us. They counted off-- I don't remember whether it was 50 or 60. And the rest of us was led down steps, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was built by our Jewish brothers and fathers. And hundreds of thousands of Jewish lives were lost building those steps in the mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mauthausen. This was for the Germans when they had to come to the gypsy camp, which was-- consisted of one barrack that was a shortcut for them not to use gasoline, just as a shortcut and precaution. And on a nice day it was a beautiful walk to make down those steps. It was the most beautiful sight you want to see, 151 or 159 steps. And then was that barrack in the most picturesque area of the Alp Mountains. It was down in a valley, with big, tall fir trees reaching the sky, surrounded by-- on the other side surrounded by a forest. And this one barrack-- I don't know how many people it housed. But there was any-- you name the nationality, it was there. There were French women, whoever was survived. There were Polish women. There were Polish, not Jewish. Because we were the Polish Jews. There were Polish women. There were Russian women. There were gypsies. As a matter of fact, the Lageralteste, the blockova, the one-- the-- the-- they call it the Haftlings. That was the incarcerated person. That was a gypsy woman. She had a German lover. And as soon as we were brought into her [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] abode, we got our pieces of straw to lie down on. She did not pull any punches. She says, they are overworked in the crematorium. So you will be gassed on Friday evening. Friday night is your day. So don't make yourself too comfortable here. You're not going to stay too long. That was the greeting that she gave us. Fine. All right. We still were getting that one-a-day soup and a slice of bread, cut according to her ideas. I don't know what the others were given. I was too weak and too sick to even be interested in knowing what others had. [SIGHS] I only know that every morning and every evening we had to stay at an Appel and be counted, and the SS people came to count us. Till this day, I don't know, and I don't understand. I think I could say I'm the only one survivor that survived Auschwitz, survived all the other camps, in eyeglasses. I don't think there was anybody else. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I have my eyeglasses with me. It was my-- it's my treasure. And when I mentioned to my daughter maybe I ought to give it to the museum, she says, no. This is ours, Eli's and mine. That's your dowry that you brought with you. Now [SIGHS] came Friday evening. And we're waiting for the trucks to take us. And we standing outside. We were not counted. We were counted by the blockova. But we're waiting for the SS man, SS woman. Nobody shows up. We only see jeeps passing by to the forest, motorcycles going by to the forest on the other road, not the road near us. But we don't see anything. We don't know anybody. We don't know anything. We're standing there. We're standing there. Now, when night came, the woman, the blockova was afraid that some of us will take a walk-- where to, I don't know. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But she was afraid of that. So she made us go back into the barrack. Because at least this way she knew where we were. They never came to count us again. Because next morning we heard-- and through the night we heard gun-- guns. We knew that-- we had the feeling that the war is coming to an end. But we did not know how and when. And we also knew that as long as the Germans are near us, we are not certain that we will survive. We are not certain that we will live, that we will be allowed to live. That somewheres along the line, they'll always find a way to grab hold of us and finish us off. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But there was no escape for us whatsoever. Now, Saturday morning, again, they lined us up to be counted. Nobody showed up. And we were-- and it was a nice spring day. We were standing outside. And the blockova wasn't there anymore. Somewheres along the night I guess her lover came for her, and they left. And around noon time, a-- a motorcycle for two stopped by our gate. SS men went out to the one who was on-- on guard, pulled off his insignias, and the other one pulled his insignias off. They got onto the motorcycle. They disappeared. [BACKGROUND NOISE] There was nobody anymore at the gate. A few hours laters-- later, the man-- Mauthausen had not only Jewish men incarcerated there. Mauthausen had Germans who were Communists, or anti-Nazis, or for whatever transgressions they did. They had Germans. They had Russians. They had gypsies, whoever survived of the gypsies. They had Jews. They had Poles-- not too many, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever was strong enough, they emptied out the warehouse with-- from food. And they cooked a very good soup. And they brought it to the women in camp. And they brought crackers that they found, and some bread that they found, which was very generous. It was very nice of them. Whoever ate got sick because we were not used to that. I recall, I took the cracker and I took a slice of bread. The soup-- it was this-- the spoon was-- I had a spoon, remember? [LAUGHS] That spoon was standing in that bowl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tasted it. And I spit it out. I just couldn't swallow it. It was too rich for-- for me. I couldn't. So I had that piece of bread, and I had a cracker. And then they brought us some coffee. And this I drank. I enjoyed that. That was enough for me. And Monday-- Sunday morning they brought us some-- whatever they could scrounge around, bread, whatever they had, and the coffee. And again, they brought the soup. But whoever ate it the first, they could not eat it any more. And that soup was diluted already. But even so, it was too-- too rich. Because they threw everything that they had, whatever possible, meats and all that. It was just too much. And the next morning the American Army came in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the first thing that they did [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they settled in the camp, and after they oversaw what was [? on, ?] the next day they came with DDT pumps, DDT guns. And God bless them for it. Because we were eaten away by the lice. And they sprayed our clothing. They sprayed the straw in the barracks. They did not let us into it. We had to get undressed. They sprayed our bodies. They sprayed our clothes. God bless them for it. Because this was-- I think if I would win now a million dollars, I don't think I would be as happy as I was as happy then when that itching stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date? Do you remember the date of your liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May 8. And May 9 I was liberated from lice. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop for a moment--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to change the tape.
